sentences.text: We didn't know that until much later, but I kept looking at her, you know.
I mean, what's there to be nervous about?
They're packing.
We're getting out of Germany.
We were, you know, as the children, my sister and I, we were excited about it.
We're leaving.
We're going to be free.
Mother was very-- my father was downstairs with my sister.
And I was upstairs with my mother.
This man who packed was an elderly-- short little elderly gentleman.
He had very kind eyes.
And I saw him looking at my mother.
My mother was such a beautiful woman.
And I saw my mother exchanging looks with him.
And I thought, oh my god.
She's got something up her sleeve again.
I don't know what it could be, but she's telling that guy something.
So it was almost to the top.
Afterwards, I thought, that poor woman.
She had a little leather pouch in her pocket with the jewelry in it.
How is she going to-- under the eyes of the Gestapo.
And she wasn't allowed to touch anything.
They didn't count-- they didn't know my mother.
It was almost to the top.
There were clothes on the top.
When my mother said, ach, Herr So and So.
This is going to be so wrinkled by the time it gets there.
And she bends over and drops it down.
And the SS, the Gestapo men pulled her back and said, you are not allowed to do that.
She said, I just smoothed it out.
The man saw what she did.
And he said, I'm sorry Frau Schohl, I'll smooth it out.
And he stuffed it deeper.
See, there were people-- I guess they felt sorry for us.
But it takes a person like my mother, the courage, I don't think I would have had that courage.
Anyhow, they were sealed.
And my mother said, 'Maxl, go with them to Frankfurt.' 'Why?
Nothing can happen.
They're going straight from Frankfurt to Genoa.'
'Go.
I want them'-- she was scared to death if they opened them.
She wants them on the train to-- and that's what happened.
And he came back and he said, Liesel, they're on their way to Genoa.
OK.
So then I don't remember exactly how many more days we stayed in the house before we left.
There were no Jewish people left in Florsheim, with the exception of that lady who went with us.
We went to her and told her we are leaving there and then.
The taxi will pick you up, pick us up.
We will drive to Frankfurt in the taxi and overnight a train to Munich.
And the next morning, we boarded the Orient Express to Zagreb, because--
My grandmother was taken with her sister to a Jewish old people's home in Frankfurt.
And as long as she was there, my father went every day.
But he came home one day and he said, you know, I'm so thankful she doesn't know where she is.
Well, there is a story before we get to Zagreb.
When we boarded the Orient Express, the conductor-- somehow Jewish immigrants, even though we looked very nice, there was something about a Jewish immigrant, you could tell.
And the conductor must have noticed that, because he was so kind.
As I said, we had-- there is a third class with wooden benches.
Second class had gray velvet.
First class had red velvet.
So we bought tickets second class.
The conductor said, I'm so sorry, but the SS has taken over all second class compartments.
I don't know what to tell you, with the exception that the first class compartments are all taken.
There is one gentleman in that one first class compartment.
Let me go and ask him if he would let you sit here.
You're getting-- he is going to Sofia and you're getting off in Zagreb.
So all we saw was a big newspaper.
And we saw the newspaper go like this.
[GESTURING] So we sat on the bench, scared to death.
We couldn't see anything because of the newspaper and we just sat.
My parents, those two faces, they were whitish-greenish.
It was so sad.
Of course, my sister and I were excited.
This was an adventure for us.
But this was their home.
This is-- you know, afterwards as I was growing up, I could feel the pain that they went through.
Mother's hand was ice cold and my father never said a word.
And we just sat there.
And all of a sudden, he folded the newspaper and put it down.
He got up-- I can't get up now-- and put his heels together and bowed and said-- I'm going to say the real name, because god only knows that he's long dead and gone-- Jean [?
Manzalia. ?] He said, I am a Greek diplomat, and he reached in his pocket, and his passport was like this.
And he said, and two days ago, I stood on the balcony with Hitler and we waved to the crowds.
He sat down and he smirked.
And he said, and you're Jewish immigrants.
And my father got up, and he clicked his heels, and he bowed.
And he said Dr.
Max Schohl, my wife, and my two daughters.
How do you do?
How do you do?
We are leaving Germany legally.
Here are our passports.
And he showed him that we had legal-- we are visiting a friend in Yugoslavia.
And my mother, I guess it just came out, said, we come from hell.
And the guy said, you better be quiet, because the next apartments are full of SS men.
They are going-- wherever they were going, I don't know.
They are accompanying actors and actresses for a play, wherever it was.
I don't remember.
So he said, ssh.
You have to be quiet.
Don't talk about it.
Let me tell you a little about myself.
We didn't trust him.
We didn't know.
We did not know.
But he kept looking at me and kept looking at me.
And he's older than my father, I don't have to worry.
Before we got to the border, he had asked my father for my hand in marriage.
As I'm sitting here, it's the truth.
And if you'd ever talk to my sister, she would say, can you imagine my sister being so dumb and saying no?
It's true.
And my father was always the gentleman, said 'I am honored, but my daughter is not even 16.' As a matter of fact, I was 16 that month. 'I would never tell my daughter who to marry and when to marry.
And I think that she is too young.' And he said, 'no, she's not.
In Greece, we would live in a white house in Saloniki, and you would never have to worry about the Nazis anymore.' And he said, 'Monsieur, I cannot sell my daughter.' And my mother kept going--
So we went out.
Do you know how these trains-- the Orient Express, you go out of the compartment and long hallway, beautiful windows.
You look out.
And so my sister and I went there.
And [?
Jean Manzalia ?] came and stood with us.
And my mother said, Maxl, you don't have to say no right away.
Just play along.
The guy can help us.
I tell you, she was terrific.
They were complimenting each other.
My father is so straightforward, and my mother always looking-- wonderful, just wonderful.
He told us he was going to Sofia and from there back to Germany and so on and so forth.
But he wants to see us in Yugoslavia.
We arrived in Zagreb at midnight.
And the strange language, you couldn't even make out words.
All you heard were sounds.
But we were told you'll get along with French, and never utter a German word.
Monsieur [?
Manzalia ?] got out of the train and said something to the conductor.
And he said, you are strangers here.
I just changed my mind.
I'm going to stay with you and see that you're settled.
My father said, we are to go across from the station is a hotel where our friend told us to go.
Anyway, I will go quickly through this.
He stayed with us two days.
Easter Sunday he showed us Zagreb.
Beautiful.
I mean, the food.
And it was a beautiful city.
And he showed us the outdoor cafes and the restaurants.
And I think we got probably sick eating all that food that we hadn't had in a long time.
Then he left, but took our address where we were at that time, so that we were to leave-- every time we left, we were to-- and we did.
It got too expense-- we got the money-- since we couldn't take out any money, my father cabled Amsterdam.
And Amsterdam sent us from the dollars money, so we had.
But it got too expensive in the hotel.
When our friend called us, he said, I am delayed.
Take a rooming house, it is cheap, and wait for me.
So that's what we did.
And we waited for him.
And when he came, he said, I'm going with you to Belgrade.
You'll have to get away.
Zagreb is too close to the border.
That's too dangerous for you.
We're going.
So he went with us to Belgrade.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
And he survived the war.
His name was [?
Yakob, ?] Jacob, [?
Altmeier. ?] We stayed in Belgrade.
Three weeks go by fast.
Our visitors' visa, my father went every day.
In the meantime, our passports went back to the Brazilian embassy.
My father went there every day, and every day they said, soon, soon.
Our visitors' visa runs out.
The trouble is, then the Yugoslav have the choice-- or maybe it was a must-- to put us back into Germany.
And we knew that.
So my father went to the B'nai B'rith, he was a member, and said, we are in a predicament.
In a few days we are going to be here illegally, and they can send-- they said, don't worry.
We have so many illegals here from Austria, from Poland, Czechoslovakia.
We are sending all of you, and you will be going with the ones who are here illegally into the interior in a little village about 2, 2 and 1/2 hours by car from Belgrade.
And it is called Ruma, R-U-M-A.
We stayed to the very end, until our visa ran out.
My father went to the Brazilian embassy and they handed us the passports back and said, Brazilian closed its doors.
We were stuck.
There was no place else to go.
So we took a taxi and drove to Ruma.
And on the way-- and I'm saying this, which sounds very unimportant, but it isn't.
My father looked out the window of the taxi.
And he said, stop a minute.
And he walked out.
There were fields and-- you know.
And he said, Liesel, look at this.
The red dirt, that's copper.
He said, god, what a chemist could do here.
We were told where to go in Ruma.
By whom?
The B'nai B'rith.
Every immigrant had the name of either one person or several persons.
Also the gathering point of all the immigrants were the synagogue courtyard.
I cannot remember the name of the rabbi.
He had seven daughters, and [?
Ubah, ?] [?
Hinko, ?] and [?
Shmula ?] and three sons.
It was so hot.
We got out of the taxi.
Ruma was bigger than Florsheim in size, but it was like suddenly you were 100 years back in time.
The streets were dirt.
Every step you made, you inhaled the dust.
And this must have been on a Saturday, because there was the market.
On Saturdays in the town square, village square, all the farmers came from all around with their produce, with their live poultry, with their little squeaking pigs.
I've never seen a cow or a lamb, while I was there.
You know that.
And the strange sound.
So we got to this man, I mean, to this person, and there was Bela Hauser.
And he spoke three languages.
They all spoke three languages.
And he said, I am going to get you into a pretty nice large-sized house which is for rent.
But for the next few days, you'll have to just make do.
I'll put you on a farm, because this farmer needs the money, has two little boys.
And it's on the outskirts of Ruma.
They had a room for rent, and so the four of us slept then for the first time.
We slept on a bed was a square.
Well, it was not a square.
It was a wooden frame, a wooden frame, a raw wooden frame, and a sack, a huge sack the size of the bed with a slit in the middle.
And it was stuffed with straw.
And every morning, you hold your breath and you went like this and-- you know.
And then was like a sack cloth came over it.
That was your bed.
And believe me, you learned to sleep on it.
You learned everything.
You learned to go-- Ruma with the exception of-- I'm trying to think who had electricity.
Very few people had electricity.
This house did not have electricity.
The house we went to later on had electricity, but no running water and no indoor facilities.
My mother said, children, there is your bathroom, outhouse.
You learn.
You learn.
And a pump.
If you wanted to wash, you go, you get a bucket of water, and you go and then you heat the water on the wooden stove.
No.
Not during that time.
Later on, when we lived alone-- it was maybe two or three weeks later that Bela Hauser came and said, we're neighbors now.
And this was a huge house.
The people who owned it went to America.
And Hauser was the representative to rent it.
They did not want to sell it, but they rented it out.
It was one of those-- I guess they would call it a mansion.
We wouldn't call it a mansion, but I guess they would.
Only that one lady.
And she was in a rooming house.
No.
No.
Not then.
Not then.
We were already in that house and we were washing windows, huge windows, on a Saturday.
And Mother said, girls, come here.
Now you can see the Gypsies.
We found out there was a Gypsy camp out on the outskirts.
They were not allowed in town.
But on Saturdays, they came into town in their very colorful clothes to sell their handmade tools, wooden spoons and that.
And Mother said, oh, God, how poor they are.
These poor, poor people.
And she said, you know what?
We've got a lot, really.
We each go to our suitcases and take out clothes.
And next Saturday when they come, I want to give it to them.
We made a big bundle of clothes.
And next Saturday, one of us hung out the window and were looking.
The Gypsies are coming!
We didn't ask anybody about the Gypsies.
Mother very well always did what she wanted to do.
And so Mother took the clothes and we followed her.
And we stood on the sidewalk.
And when they came, Mother went-- and they went back.
No, no.
No, no, no.
They had a name for stealing.
And if-- and my mother looked and took the oldest one, took her arm, and said, [POINTING] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
No, no.
Well, anyhow we finally got them.
And they kept looking back [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
They couldn't get over it.
This was the first time that somebody had shown them kindness.
A few nights later-- there was a big yard in the back-- we heard the most beautiful music coming from our yard.
And so we walked through the window, kitchen window in the back.
There was a Gypsy standing in the yard playing the violin, beautiful.
My father said, that is as good as I have ever heard it.
And he very quietly went down the steps into the yard.
There were chairs and tables there.
And he sat down.
We stayed upstairs.
And when he had finished, my father got up and went over and shook hands with him.
The man spoke-- I mean, he not only spoke Serbian and French.
They spoke French together.
But he also spoke German and Hungarian.
He went to the University of Vienna.
When his father, who was the king, died, he was called back.
He didn't have to.
He said, I don't have to do this.
My father said, my god.
That man is an educated person.
He choose to do this for his people, hoping that by his being educated and by his having seen other life that he could improve--
No.
His first name was [?
Yanush ?].
But I don't remember the other name.
And--
Yes, yes I have.
But he came almost every night.
And Father would bring out wine, and they would sit, and he would play, and then they would talk.
And my father said, tell me, why are the Gypsies so hated and so [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is the Jewish word?
And he said, because there are different tribes.
And some of the tribes, yes, they steal.
Yes, they lie.
Yes, they cheat.
Yes, they are very bad.
He said, I won't allow this in my tribe.
He said, my tribe, he said-- oh, the very first time my father said, thank you.
Thank you.
What a treat.
And he said, no.
Thank you for being-- treating our women like they're human beings.
He said, we are blessing your wife every day.
And he said, if you ever need us, we will be here for you.
And you never have to be afraid when the Gypsies are in town.
We will look after you.
And every Saturday, when they came, they-- the women.
Yes.
The encounter, that I have to go ahead--
In that house?
This was 1940.
The German occupation came April, 1941.
Until probably the end of-- it could be the end of '41, the beginning of '42.
I don't exactly remember.
We were taken to a place where they could watch us better.
The German police.
Now, I have to explain something to you.
When the Germans-- when the Germans came to occupy, I don't know what year it was, Austria had a queen, Maria Theresa.
And she took some of her people and settled them in what is now Croatia.
And these Germans, they were called Volksdeutsche.
Their ancestors came from Austria.
They considered themselves German although they couldn't hardly speak German.
They couldn't write.
Most of them couldn't write at all.
They were farmers.
And they grew up in Yugoslavia, as did their parents, as did their grandparents.
Now, all of a sudden with the German occupation, they were the good Germans.
And the men couldn't wait to join not the German army, but a different army with a different uniform.
And they were called the Volksdeutsche police.
They were worse than the SS and the Gestapo, because these dumb, ignorant, uneducated people wanted to show the big bosses in Berlin, we can kill them too, and better than you can.
And it was these people who came.
They wore black uniforms like the SS and the black cap.
But they had an insignia which showed they were not the Reich German, but the Volks German.
Yes, they came.
And they said, you have to move, and so--
At that particular time, if you still had feeling left, I suppose you were.
But really and truly, by that time, I don't think we had any feelings left.
Sometimes I think I'm going to stick myself to see if I have any blood left.
That's-- that was a bad time.
All right.
Before I go into the other house, we were still in that house when the Gestapo came.
The Gestapos had their headquarters in [?
Samoon, ?] or [?
Samleen. ?] One says [?
Samoon, ?] the other says [?
Samleen. ?] And they would make trips to the surrounding places, little towns and villages, where Jews were.
And this particular Sunday-- it was on a Sunday.
This particular Sunday, the Gestapo was here.
And a neighbor, a young man who dated my sister, came and said the Gestapo is here, and there is a cattle car at the railroad station.
And we sent him out again, find out what's happening to the other Jews.
So--
He did.
